concept_name: Lorentz Force on a Moving Charged Particle
notes: Voice-professor doubt bundle. Haiku CLASSIFIES a spoken doubt into one of these clusters; the answer_text is served verbatim (Rule 17/18 — no live physics generation). Every answer is hand-authored and MUST be founder-reviewed before any student use. jump_to_state values are validated against field_3d_config.states at load time; glow tokens must be in the field_3d_renderer SET_GLOW vocabulary (v|f|b|v_parallel|v_perp|trail|hand|fleming|sliders|fleming_index|fleming_middle|fleming_thumb).

fallback.label: fallback
fallback.answer_text: That's a good question. Let me bring up the key picture for this so we can look at it together.

gated_states.note: Fleming's LEFT-hand rule (Class-10 reconciliation aside). Only show when the student explicitly asks about the left hand or Fleming — never on a right-hand-rule question (use STATE_2 / STATE_6, the right-hand states, for those).

clusters.label: Does the force speed it up?
clusters.description: Student thinks a magnetic field can increase the particle's speed or add energy.
trigger_examples: does the magnetic field make it go faster
does a stronger field make it move faster
can the field give it more energy
clusters.answer_text: No — a magnetic force can only steer the charge, never speed it up. The force stays perpendicular to the velocity, so it does zero work and the speed is locked. A stronger field just makes a tighter circle.

clusters.label: Why is the path a circle?
clusters.description: Student asks why the trajectory comes out circular when v is perpendicular to B.
clusters.answer_text: Because the force is always perpendicular to the velocity and constant in size, it always points to one centre. A steady sideways pull at constant speed is exactly what bends a path into a circle.

clusters.label: How does the right-hand rule work?
clusters.description: Student wants the procedure for finding the direction of the magnetic force.
trigger_examples: how do I find the direction of the force
which way does the force point
how do I use my hand for this
clusters.answer_text: Point your right-hand fingers along v, curl them toward B, and your thumb gives the force. Fingers first, thumb last. For a negative charge, the force points the opposite way.

clusters.label: Is the force along the motion?
clusters.description: Student believes the force pushes the particle forward, in the direction of v.
trigger_examples: isn't the force in the direction it's moving
does the force push it forward
does the field push it ahead
clusters.answer_text: No — the force is never along the motion. It is the cross product of v and B, so it sits at ninety degrees to v. That is why it turns the particle instead of speeding it up.

clusters.label: Is the force along the field?
clusters.description: Student believes the force points along the magnetic field B.
trigger_examples: is the force along the field
is F in the direction of B
does the field pull it along the field lines
clusters.answer_text: No — the force is perpendicular to B as well. The part of the velocity along B feels nothing; only the part across B makes a force. So F is never along the field.

clusters.label: What is theta?
clusters.description: Student asks what the angle theta in the formula refers to.
trigger_examples: what does the angle mean here
clusters.answer_text: Theta is the angle between the velocity and the magnetic field. At ninety degrees the force is strongest and you get a full circle. At zero degrees they are parallel and there is no force.

clusters.label: What happens at other angles?
clusters.description: Student asks about angles between 0 and 90 degrees and why the path becomes a helix.
trigger_examples: what if it's at forty five degrees
why does it become a spiral
clusters.answer_text: Between zero and ninety the circle opens into a helix. The part of the velocity along B drifts forward while the part across B keeps circling. A smaller angle gives a longer, more stretched-out spiral.

clusters.label: Why sin theta in the formula?
clusters.description: Student thinks sin theta is an arbitrary correction factor to memorise.
trigger_examples: where does the sin come from
why is there a sin in the formula
is sin theta just a correction
clusters.answer_text: Sin theta is not magic. Split the velocity into a part along B and a part across B. Only the across part, which is v times sin theta, makes the force. So F is really q times that perpendicular slice times B.

clusters.label: Isn't it the left-hand rule?
clusters.description: Student asks about Fleming's left-hand rule versus the right-hand cross product.
trigger_examples: what about Fleming's left hand rule
do I use my left hand
clusters.answer_text: Fleming's left-hand rule works too for a positive charge — index along B, middle along v, thumb gives force. We prefer the right-hand cross product because it handles any angle and negative charges without extra rules.

clusters.label: Radius and time period
clusters.description: Student asks for the radius of the circle or the time period of one loop.
trigger_examples: how long does one loop take
clusters.answer_text: The radius is m v over q B — faster particles make bigger circles. But the period, two pi m over q B, does not depend on speed at all. Every loop takes the same time.

state_hints.STATE_1: theta_deg, v, B, q_sign — straight (θ≈0) baseline; raise θ to bend the path
state_hints.STATE_2: theta_deg, v, B, q_sign — circle at θ=90; LOWER θ to open the circle into a helix; raise B for a tighter/faster circle; flip q_sign to reverse the circulation
state_hints.STATE_3: theta_deg, v, B, q_sign — constant-speed circle; same knobs
state_hints.STATE_5: theta_deg, v, B, q_sign — helix at θ=45; change θ to reshape the helix
state_hints.STATE_6: theta_deg, v, B, q_sign — decomposition at θ=45; change θ to grow/shrink v⊥ (the slice that makes F)
state_hints.STATE_7: theta_deg, v, B, q_sign — stretched helix at θ=10; raise θ toward 90 to tighten it into a circle
state_hints.STATE_8: theta_deg, v, B, q_sign — interactive sliders on screen; turning a knob also moves its slider

moves.label: circle to helix demo
moves.description: Marquee gesture: perfect circle → announce the force → sweep the angle so the circle opens into a helix → tighter field → flip the charge. Bound to the F2 hero button (offline-safe).

sequence.say: Here a charged particle moves straight across the magnetic field, so it traces a perfect circle.
This is how strong the magnetic force is right now.
Now watch closely as I slowly tilt the angle down — the circle opens up into a helix.
A stronger field winds that helix tighter.
And if I flip the charge to negative, it spirals the opposite way — the same law, F equals q v cross B, the whole time.

moves.label: focus on the force
moves.description: Dim everything except the force and velocity to show F stays perpendicular to v.

sequence.say: Let me fade everything except the force and the velocity.
See how the force always sits at ninety degrees to the velocity, pointing straight to the centre.

moves.label: explain the full concept
moves.description: The standardized, reviewed full-concept performance. Walks all 8 states in the authored order (0° → 90° → no-work → Fleming → 45° → why sinθ → 10° → interactive), narrating each beat while driving the sim — face-on circle, slow-motion equal-arc trail, the two θ-sweep climaxes (circle opens into a helix, helix stretches), and the v-decomposition. Served verbatim and identical for every student. Bound to the 'Explain full concept' button via intent=explain_whole.

sequence.say: Let me walk you through how a magnetic field steers a moving charge. Blue arrows are the field B; the orange arrow is the velocity v. Right now v points along B, so theta is zero. Since F equals q v B sine theta and sine zero is zero, there is no force — the proton drifts straight.

Now turn v to ninety degrees, perpendicular to B. Sine ninety is one, so the force is maximum and always points to the centre, bending the path into a perfect circle. Newton's law gives radius r = m v over q B and period T = two pi m over q B — independent of speed.

Here is the big idea. The force stays perpendicular to the velocity, so it does zero work. Watch the trail in slow motion — the dots stay equally spaced, so the speed never changes. A magnetic force only steers a charge; it can never speed it up or slow it down.

A quick bridge for Class-ten students: some of you know this as Fleming's left-hand rule — index along B, middle along v, thumb gives F. For a positive charge it gives the exact same direction. We prefer the right hand because it works for any angle and any charge. Same physics.

Now tilt v back to forty-five degrees, and watch the closed circle open up into a helix. Part of v drifts forward along B while the rest keeps circling around it. The force is still here, but it has dropped to about seventy-one percent of its maximum.

But where does that sine theta come from? Break v into two pieces. The part along B, v cosine theta, makes no force — it only carries the proton forward. Only the perpendicular part, v sine theta, curls and makes the force. So F equals q times v sine theta times B.

Push theta down to ten degrees — v almost parallel to B. The force is still perpendicular to both, but small: sine ten is about zero point one seven. The helix stretches out, with long forward drift and tiny circles. Nudge it to zero and the circles vanish — a straight line again.

Now it is your turn. Use the four sliders — charge sign, speed, field, and angle — and watch the path respond live. Try theta zero for a straight line, ninety for a circle, anything in between for a helix. Whenever you have a question, just ask me.
